incorrects.description: Student selected 2: counted items instead of groups

steps.text: You counted what's inside. Count the circles themselves. Each circle is one group.
Let's count together. Point to each circle as you count. How many circles do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 6: may have miscounted or confused groups with total items

steps.text: Focus on the circles, not what's inside them. Each circle is one group. Count the circles.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Circles light up one by one as system counts: 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count each circle: 1, 2, 3, 4. There are 4 circles. Those are the groups. The answer is 4.

incorrects.description: Student selected 1: may be looking at first item or miscounting

params.description: First circle highlights with its 2 dots visible.

steps.text: Look inside one circle. Count the dots you see. There are more than 1.

incorrects.description: Student selected 3: miscounted items in a group

params.description: First circle highlights with its 2 dots visible.

steps.text: Here's what helps. Pick one circle. Count just the dots inside that circle carefully.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4: counted total groups instead of items per group

params.description: First circle highlights with its 2 dots visible.

steps.text: You counted all the circles. I asked what's inside ONE circle. Look at just one and count the dots.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: First circle highlights. Dots inside pulse as system counts: 1, 2.

steps.text: This one takes a bit of thought, so let's work through it together. I look inside one circle. I count the dots: 1, 2. Each group has 2 dots. The answer is 2.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 groups of 4: reversed the numbers

steps.text: You switched the numbers. Count the circles first. That tells you how many groups. Then count inside one circle.

incorrects.description: Student selected 4 groups of 4: repeated the group count

params.description: First circle highlights with 2 dots visible.

steps.text: You said 4 twice. The first number is right: 4 circles. But look inside one circle. How many dots do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 2 groups of 2: used items per group for both numbers

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Count all the circles. That's your first number. Then count inside one circle for the second number.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Circles highlight one at a time as system counts groups, then one circle zooms to show 2 dots.

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. First I count the circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. That's 4 groups. Then I look inside one circle and count: 1, 2. That's 2 in each group. So I have 4 groups of 2. The answer is 4 groups of 2.
